stories.title: 11-year-olds mowed 50 lawns for free so the ‘Lawnmower Man’ donated gear to open a business
stories.summary: Two 11-year-olds in Alabama took on the 50 Yard Challenge, mowing lawns for free for those in need. When Rodney Smith Jr., the Lawnmower Man, heard they were sharing a donated mower, he drove down and gifted them brand-new equipment. Now the boys are turning their hard work into a real business.
stories.extended_summary: When Ja’Torrian Taylor and Tevin Rice signed up for the 50 Yard Challenge, they didn't expect a reward — they just wanted to help their community. After mowing 50 lawns for free, Rodney Smith Jr. showed up with a mower, blower, and trimmer for each of them. "This could be the beginning of a successful lawn service," he said. The boys' grit paid off, and their new gear is taking their business to the next level.

stories.title: 25 Years Later: Lessons Learned from the Martis Fire
stories.summary: Twenty-five years after the Martis Fire nearly destroyed a California town, one man's foresight stands out. Darin Bue spent years clearing defensible space against neighbors' pushback. When the fire came, his property became a staging ground that saved Floriston.
stories.extended_summary: In 2001, the Martis Fire raged through Truckee, burning 14,000 acres and threatening the tiny community of Floriston. But firefighters found a haven on Darin Bue's land — he'd been cutting and burning brush for a decade, even when neighbors were skeptical. "I had an instinct about it," Bue says. That instinct, and years of hard work, turned his hillside into a firebreak that helped save the town. The lesson in preparedness still echoes today.
stories.headline_split: 25 Years Later:

stories.title: 30,000 Scottish Descend on Boston for World Cup–And the Amazing Viral Videos Remind Us We’re All Neighbors
stories.summary: When 30,000 Scottish fans descended on Boston for the World Cup, they brought bagpipes, endless energy, and a party that locals couldn't resist. One neighbor grilled sausages for the crew at 6 a.m., and soon the whole city was chanting along. The Tartan Army left behind empty kegs and new friendships.
stories.extended_summary: At 6:30 a.m., bagpipes rang out in a quiet Boston suburb as Scottish fans arrived at their Airbnb. Instead of complaints, neighbor Mike Morrison fired up the grill and joined the fun. The camaraderie went viral — now he's headed to Miami to reunite with the crew. Meanwhile, a local taproom had to order four emergency deliveries after fans downed 4,000 pints. The World Cup turned Boston into a celebration of cross-cultural joy.
stories.headline_split: 30,000 Scottish Descend on Boston for World Cup–And the Amazing Viral Videos Remind Us

stories.title: A group of Somerville artists no longer has to fear eviction
stories.summary: When the owners decided to sell their Somerville building, the artists inside feared displacement. Instead, they teamed up with a nonprofit to buy the studios for $1.6 million, securing affordable workspace for 25 creatives. Now they can focus on art, not rent.
stories.extended_summary: In an expensive city like Boston, artists often worry about losing their studios. But at Central Street Studios, a novel solution turned that worry into relief. After a multi-year fundraiser, the Arts & Business Council bought the building, preserving it as affordable workspace. "This is one piece of stability within a life that can feel unstable," said musician Cara Giaimo. The ribbon-cutting celebrated not just a building, but a community's determination to stay put.

stories.title: A new chapter for an old house in Newport begins on Juneteenth
stories.summary: Under the floorboards of Newport's oldest house, a bundle of objects revealed a hidden history: a nkisi, a spiritual collection from west-central Africa. Now the house opens as a Black History Center on Juneteenth, honoring the enslaved people who lived and worked there.
stories.extended_summary: Twenty-one years ago, renovation of the Wanton-Lyman-Hazard House uncovered a blue-and-white cloth bundle containing a cowry shell, a peach pit, and a human tooth. It was an nkisi — a spiritual object linking the home's enslaved residents to African traditions. That discovery sparked years of community conversations, culminating in a new center that opens Juneteenth 2026. The house's story now includes the full truth of who lived there, and the resilience they carried.
stories.headline_split: A new chapter for an old house in Newport begins on
stories.fake_headline: Abandoned Train Station Transformed into Vibrant Community Center

stories.title: A place called home: Village Hearth
stories.summary: Village Hearth in Durham is more than a neighborhood — it's a cohousing community designed by and for LGBTQ+ older adults. With front porches facing each other and a culture of stepping up, residents have found belonging and support.
stories.extended_summary: When residents forgot bowls for dinner at Village Hearth, neighbors didn't wait to be asked — they grabbed extra bowls from their own homes. That small gesture captures the spirit of this LGBTQ+ senior cohousing community. Founded by Margaret Roesch and Pat McAulay, the 28-home village keeps cars on the perimeter so people walk and talk. "You help them with groceries. You know each other," says resident Janet Svoboda. It's a place where connection is built into the design.

stories.title: A ‘bottom-up’ and ‘in-house’ approach to AI workforce training gains traction in San Jose
stories.summary: San Jose is tackling the AI skills gap with a bold upskilling program. Over 1,000 city workers have already learned to build their own AI tools. The goal: 30% of the workforce trained by 2027.
stories.extended_summary: Governments want to use AI, but many workers lack tech backgrounds. San Jose's AI Upskilling Program flips that — teaching employees to create custom AI solutions for their specific jobs. Since 2024, 15% of the city's workforce has completed it. The program is a "force multiplier" for a lean staff serving nearly a million residents. Next target: 30% participation by 2027. It's a bottom-up approach that puts power in the hands of the people who know the work best.
stories.headline_split: A ‘bottom-up’ and ‘in-house’ approach to AI workforce training gains traction in

stories.title: After Six-Year Journey Back, St. Mel’s Catholic Academy Celebrates a Graduating Class in Queens
stories.summary: St. Mel's Catholic Academy in Queens was slated to close in 2020. Instead, it became an early education center and rebuilt grade by grade. This year, its first eighth-grade class in six years graduated — a symbol of the school's rebirth.
stories.extended_summary: At Citi Field, 11 eighth-graders celebrated more than a graduation — they celebrated a comeback. St. Mel's was almost shuttered, but leadership transformed it into an early education center and added a grade each year. "It's a rebirth," said Principal Lauren Giacalone. Graduate Marissa Neccio, who has been at the school her whole life, called it "bittersweet" to see how much everyone grew. The evening ended with Mass, games, and a pitch from the pastor. The school's future is as bright as its graduates.
stories.headline_split: After Six-Year Journey Back, St. Mel’s Catholic Academy Celebrates a Graduating Class in
stories.fake_headline: Public School in Detroit Wins National Robotics Championship After Zero Funding

stories.title: All Are Welcome
stories.summary: Eugene Pride is making the festival more accessible than ever. This year, organizers gave out over 50 discounted or free vendor spots to smaller groups, ensuring that big budgets don't decide who gets a table.
stories.extended_summary: Nick West, Eugene Pride's president, wanted to change the usual pattern: "Pride shouldn't only be accessible to organizations with big pocketbooks." So 2026's festival, happening June 27, offers more than 50 discounted or free spaces for small vendors. With queer choirs, drag performances from The Glamazons, a bounce gymnastics routine, and an afterparty with DJ Enriquedamnit, the day promises joy for everyone. Come for the march, stay for the karaoke — and know that this Pride is built for all.

stories.fake_headline: Little League Team Adopts Rules That Ensure Every Kid Gets to Play

stories.title: Bees are buzzing on Michigan State’s campus
stories.summary: Michigan State University is turning 22 acres of rarely-used lawn into wildflower meadows for bees, butterflies, and birds. The project cuts carbon emissions from mowing by letting nature take over, saving money on maintenance while creating vital pollinator habitat.
stories.extended_summary: Forget the roar of lawnmowers — Michigan State is trading manicured grass for buzzing meadows. Landscape services coordinator Josh Ridner teamed up with an entomologist to identify 22 acres of campus lawn that nobody actually uses, stopped mowing them, and planted native pollinator-friendly flowers like butterfly weed and blazing star. The result is a haven for bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds, lower maintenance costs, and a dramatic cut in planet-warming emissions. Ridner expects the project to pay for itself within three years.

stories.title: Big Rube’s Philly: Jamele Ransom, Crossing Guard and Then Some
stories.summary: When a car crashed into a schoolyard in Southwest Philly, crossing guard Jamele Ransom didn't think twice—he ran toward the danger and pulled a child out from under crushed monkey bars. His viral interview, complete with a Mister Softee ice cream cone, earned him 15 million views and a shoutout from Jimmy Kimmel.
stories.extended_summary: On the last day of school, crossing guard Jamele Ransom heard a crash and saw a car barreling toward the schoolyard. Without a second thought, he dodged the vehicle and pulled a trapped child from mangled playground equipment. Afterward, shaking and emotional, he accepted an ice cream cone from a Mister Softee truck and gave an interview that racked up 15 million views. Jimmy Kimmel even gave him a shoutout — but Jamele says he was just being himself.

stories.title: Black Women Farmers Are Reclaiming the Land
stories.summary: Black women farmers are reclaiming their ancestral connection to the land at Soul Fire Farm in upstate New York. Through immersive programs, they learn traditional farming and herbal remedies while passing down knowledge that has sustained communities for generations.
stories.extended_summary: On a June morning at Soul Fire Farm, two dozen participants are learning to plant and harvest on land that once belonged to the Mohican people. Tour guide Hillary Gaeta points out rows of mint and lemon balm, laughing as she recalls grandmas who look 70 but have been creating herbal remedies for decades. The week-long program teaches Black, Indigenous, and people of color the ancestral farming skills that have been passed down through generations — skills that Leah Penniman says are often overlooked, even though Black women grow most of the world's food on small farms.

stories.fake_headline: Black Women Chefs Open Community Kitchen in Food Desert

stories.title: Breck honors Gene Dayton, visionary co-founder of Breck Nordic and BOEC
stories.summary: Before snowcats and fancy equipment, Gene Dayton strapped a rubber tire to his waist and dragged it behind him to groom ski trails. Decades later, the 83-year-old visionary was honored for founding Breckenridge Nordic Center and pioneering adaptive outdoor recreation for people of all abilities.
stories.extended_summary: In the 1970s, Gene Dayton didn't have a snowcat to groom trails at his new Nordic center — so he strapped an old tire around his waist and skied laps, flattening the snow behind him. That do-it-yourself spirit never left him. Over the decades, he and his wife Therese built Breckenridge Nordic Center and the Breckenridge Outdoor Education Center, creating opportunities for people of all ages and abilities to ski, hike, and experience the freedom of nature. At 83, Gene received the town's Community Leadership Award, cracking a knowing smile as the council shared his tire-dragging story.
stories.headline_split: Breck honors Gene Dayton,

stories.fake_headline: Aspen Honors Local Ski Patroller Who Saved 200 Lives

stories.title: Bret Michaels Helps To Set New ‘Golden Guitars Gala’ Record In Rapid City
stories.summary: Bret Michaels surprised the crowd at the Golden Guitars Gala in Rapid City, helping raise a record-breaking $781,250 for children's health. The rock legend performed acoustically and auctioned off signed guitars, pushing the event past its previous fundraising highs.
stories.extended_summary: The fifth annual Golden Guitars Gala in Rapid City wasn't expecting a rock star — but Bret Michaels showed up anyway. The Poison frontman played an intimate acoustic set and helped drive bidding on signed guitars, including a one-of-a-kind package with backstage access to his own show. By night's end, the event had raised $781,250 for Monument Health and the Children's Miracle Network — the highest single-event total in the foundation's history.

stories.title: Bring in the bears: ABR gets go-ahead to resume business
stories.summary: Appalachian Bear Rescue is back in business after being shut down for months following a tragic euthanasia order. The facility can now house up to six orphaned bear cubs at a time, with two new outdoor enclosures ready for occupancy.
stories.extended_summary: After a devastating shutdown that saw 13 cubs euthanized due to infection fears, Appalachian Bear Rescue in Townsend, Tennessee, has finally gotten the green light to resume its work. The nonprofit spent months deep-cleaning enclosures and testing soil, and now two brand-new half-acre outdoor enclosures are ready for orphaned black bear cubs. Executive Director Greg Grieco says they'll start with a maximum of six cubs at a time, but hopes to eventually expand to 18 once all enclosures pass inspection.

stories.title: Building financial confidence from the inside out
stories.summary: Inside a U.S. Bank branch in North Minneapolis, financial coach Achante Brown helps people build credit and savings for free. The Operation HOPE program has already worked with over 230 clients, turning financial anxiety into confidence and opportunity.
stories.extended_summary: For many in North Minneapolis, financial advice has always felt distant or untrustworthy — until they meet Achante Brown. Located inside the rebuilt U.S. Bank branch on West Broadway, the Operation HOPE coaching office offers free, one-on-one help with credit, savings, and small business planning. Brown grew up in the neighborhood and spent nearly a decade at U.S. Bank herself, so she knows exactly what her clients are up against. So far she's guided more than 230 people toward their financial goals, proving that trust and community connection can be just as valuable as any bank account.
stories.headline_split: Building financial confidence
stories.fake_headline: Young Entrepreneurs Launch Free Financial Literacy App for Teens

stories.title: California Is Turning Polluted Industrial Sites into Affordable Housing
stories.summary: California is turning polluted former gas stations and dry cleaners into affordable housing. In Emeryville, a new 7-story building named after local hero Nellie Hannon provides 90 homes for seniors and people exiting homelessness.
stories.extended_summary: Nellie Hannon has spent decades feeding her community through a food bank in Emeryville, California. Now, at 80 years old, she's got a new building named after her — a seven-story affordable housing complex rising on a former industrial site that needed environmental cleanup. The Nellie Hannon Gateway provides 90 homes for up to 140 people, with 39 units reserved for those leaving homelessness. The food bank she still runs occupies the ground floor, and a Native American Health Center in Oakland has opened a similar project with 76 units and a dental clinic.

stories.fake_headline: California Converts Old Prison into Tech Hub for Students

stories.title: Can kids learn to use AI without touching a screen? This Chicago after-school program is betting on it
stories.summary: A Chicago after-school program called Overture Games is teaching kids to use AI without screens — using paper, pencils, and brainstorming. Students write song lyrics by hand, then watch an AI tool bring their music to life.
stories.extended_summary: Eight-year-old Matthew Uriosdegui and his friends wanted to write a song for their last day of after-school program — so they grabbed pencils, brainstormed lyrics on paper, and decided on a groovy piano tune about an island party. Their instructor typed their handwritten notes into Suno, an AI music generator, and within seconds the kids were snapping along to their own creation. Overture Games is bringing that kind of magic to hundreds of students across Illinois and Massachusetts, teaching AI literacy through hands-on, screen-free activities that start with imagination, not clicks.

stories.title: Charleston dad turns a simple newsletter into a nationwide community for fathers
stories.summary: When Adam Kunes became a dad, he couldn't find fatherhood content that felt real — so he created Dad Day. The newsletter has grown into a nationwide community of 20,000 dads with meetups, run clubs, and honest conversations about parenting.
stories.extended_summary: Adam Kunes felt isolated as a new dad — his friends were still out at bars while he was learning baby sleep schedules. The online content for fathers felt either cheesy or insulting, so the Charleston entrepreneur started Dad Day, a newsletter that quickly exploded. Three times a week, it covers everything from parenting and fitness to marriage and humor, and has spawned in-person meetups, run clubs, and a Slack group. Within months, 20,000 dads across the country had joined, proving that a lot of guys were looking for the exact same thing: a community that lets them stay true to themselves while figuring out fatherhood.

stories.title: City of Chester fights illegal dumping with new greening initiative for vacant lots
stories.summary: Chester turned a former illegal dumping ground into a green space, part of a city-wide initiative to create accessible parks within a five-minute walk for residents. The lot at 9th and Morton, once an eyesore and even a U-Haul rental spot, now has trees and grass, giving the community a place to enjoy.
stories.extended_summary: A U-Haul rental business once operated on a gravel lot that was a haven for abandoned cars. Now that same corner in Chester has been transformed into grass and trees—one of ten vacant lots turned into green spaces through a partnership with the Pennsylvania Horticultural Society. Mayor Stefan Roots says every resident deserves a park within a five-minute walk, and Chester's new greenspaces are making that a reality.

stories.title: Clean Earth Rovers Remove Tons of Trash from the Marina
stories.summary: Clean Earth Rovers deploy autonomous robots to collect trash from Southern California marinas before it reaches the ocean. Each rover can haul up to 150 pounds of debris per bag and clean 25 acres in an eight-hour shift, making waterway cleanup efficient and impactful.
stories.extended_summary: Watching a Clean Earth Rover glide across the Cabrillo Way Marina, you'd almost mistake it for a pontoon boat—until you see the collection system sucking up trash. The company's CEO, Michael Arens, created these autonomous surface vessels to tackle marine pollution at the source. Each bag catches up to 150 pounds of debris and empties in under five minutes, allowing a single rover to cover 25 acres in a day. The Port of Los Angeles now has four rovers scouring all 15 of its marinas, preventing tons of trash from ever reaching the open ocean.

stories.title: Close-up with Quintin Washington
stories.summary: Quintin Washington delivers food by bike seven days a week, but also runs a YouTube channel where he's conducted over 3,200 community interviews over 14 years—all for free. His passion project has turned him into a local celebrity, and he says it's a public service, not a paycheck.
stories.extended_summary: While most delivery drivers focus on the next order, Quintin Washington is juggling his bike route with interview schedules. He's broadcast more than 3,200 discussions on his YouTube channel, Quintin's Close-Ups, over the last 14 years—all without earning a dime. If he has an interview during a delivery shift, he'll pause Uber to chat, then hop back on his bike. Washington calls it a public service, and his growing following across platforms shows Charleston agrees.

stories.title: Club in correctional facility donated $1,200 to local food pantry
stories.summary: Inmates at Deer Ridge Correctional Institution raised $1,200 for a local food pantry through a sandwich fundraiser. When sales only hit $700, club members chipped in their own money to reach the goal, aiming to show care for their community from inside the prison.
stories.extended_summary: A sandwich sale in La Pine initially brought in just $700 for the Madras Community Food Pantry. But the Positive Action Club at Deer Ridge Correctional Institution wanted to do more—so members contributed their own funds to push the donation to $1,200. Club president Sam Hannah said he was moved to see guys who weren't even part of the fundraiser pitching in. For these incarcerated men, the act of giving helps 'humanize and normalize' their role in the broader community.

stories.title: Comment on On Juneteenth, Ribbon Cut On Dixwell Plaza Redev by handsomerdan
stories.summary: On Juneteenth, New Haven celebrated the grand opening of First Haven in Dixwell—a 65,000-square-foot building housing a health clinic, daycare, and job training center. The project aims to restore the historic 'Black Wall Street' and bring opportunity back to the neighborhood.
stories.extended_summary: Hundreds cheered as a ribbon was cut on Juneteenth at the site of the former Dixwell Plaza, once known as New Haven's Black Wall Street. The new First Haven building is home to a medical clinic, a daycare, and a workforce training center offering phlebotomy, biotech, and culinary programs. 'This building is a declaration that hope belongs here,' said Erik Clemons, whose organization ConnCORP helped make it happen. The celebration marked a turning point for a community that's been taking itself back block by block.

stories.title: Dixon presented the President’s Award after decades of service
stories.summary: Charlie Dixon, a veteran volunteer firefighter from Thomasville, received the President's Award at the Alabama Association of Volunteer Fire Departments' conference. He helped create a retirement annuity program for volunteers that now holds over $1.5 million—starting with just $20 from his own pocket.
stories.extended_summary: When Charlie Dixon's name was called for the President's Award, he was completely speechless—a rare moment for the longtime volunteer firefighter. Decades ago, Dixon helped establish an annuity and benefits program for Alabama's volunteer firefighters, starting with a $20 contribution from his own pocket. That program now serves volunteers with over $1.5 million in retirement funds. The award, selected solely by the association's president, recognized work that Dixon himself had nearly forgotten, but that continues to support firefighters across the state.

stories.title: Door G Opens the Door for Creatives in Rhode Island
stories.summary: Steve Rosa founded Door G, a virtual production studio in East Providence, to keep creative jobs in Rhode Island. The studio is the only one on the East Coast with a Green Seal certification for sustainable production, welcoming projects of all scales.
stories.extended_summary: Steve Rosa always dreamed big while growing up in the smallest state, but he didn't want to leave Rhode Island to pursue film. So he built a place for those dreams: first (add)ventures, a branding firm, and now Door G, a virtual production studio. The studio is the only East Coast facility to earn the Environmental Media Association's Green Seal for sustainable production—using energy-efficient bulbs, composting, and eliminating plastic water bottles. Rosa's goal is to give creatives of all scales a space to turn ideas into reality, right in his home state.

stories.title: Energy Access In Rural North Carolina Grows Through Solar
stories.summary: In a mobile home community near Asheville, a partnership installed solar panels to provide energy independence and prevent displacement. The project combines affordable housing preservation with clean energy, helping residents stay in their homes despite rising utility costs.
stories.extended_summary: When Hurricane Helene hit, the PODER Emma cooperative in Asheville became a hub for resources—but it was running on diesel. Now, thanks to Sugar Hollow Solar and Footprint Project, 15 solar panels sit on the community's roof, generating power and saving money. Board member Alan Ramirez said the solar installation makes the community feel 'stronger than ever.' The project is part of a larger effort to use cooperative ownership and clean energy to protect legacy residents from displacement in a rapidly changing neighborhood.

stories.title: Fashion interest in nuns’ habits opens door to Broadway for Boise theater professor
stories.summary: Theater professor Darrin Pufall Purdy reconstructed nuns' habits for a play and ended up creating historic patterns for the Sisters of Charity, helping them reclaim a lost part of their identity. His work may now lead to a Broadway project.
stories.extended_summary: Costume designer Darrin Pufall Purdy was researching habits for the play 'Doubt' when he realized no patterns existed—the Sisters of Charity had stopped wearing them in the 1970s. He painstakingly recreated the garments from photos, then gave the patterns back to the congregation. That act of historical research sparked interest from other religious orders and opened a door to Broadway. Purdy said he found 'elegance and mystery' in the folds of the black cloth, and his work now helps nuns reconnect with a visual piece of their heritage.

stories.title: Father's Day Was Created By a Woman From Central Washington
stories.summary: Father's Day was created by Sonora Smart Dodd, who was raised by her widowed father on a farm near Wilbur, Washington. Inspired by Mother's Day, she campaigned for a holiday honoring fathers, and the first celebration was held in Spokane in 1910.
stories.extended_summary: Most people don't know that Father's Day started with a young woman from central Washington. Sonora Smart Dodd was just a girl when her Civil War veteran father raised her and four siblings alone. After hearing a Mother's Day sermon in 1909, she decided fathers deserved their own day. She rallied churches, the YMCA, and merchants in Spokane, and the first Father's Day was celebrated on June 19, 1910. It took decades of advocacy before President Nixon made it a permanent national holiday, but Dodd's determination laid the foundation for a tradition celebrated across the world.

stories.title: Fighting period poverty and menstrual ignorance, one pad at a time
stories.summary: A community in California sews reusable menstrual pads for girls in rural Uganda, where period poverty causes high dropout rates. With 2,600 pieces prepped, each pad is a step toward equality and education.
stories.extended_summary: On a Sunday afternoon, volunteers cut cotton cloth into reusable pads destined for Uganda, where 22% of girls miss school due to period issues. Rabbi Shoshana Nambi led a trip there, witnessing the cost, stigma, and fear that keep girls at home. The Casa Uganda Foundation's initiative aims to change that — one pad at a time.

stories.title: Florida woman competes in Transplant Games of America 30 years after rare lifesaving surgery
stories.summary: Kim Jacques, a heart and double-lung transplant recipient, heads to the Transplant Games of America 30 years after her rare surgery. She calls it a 'family reunion' for those who share the gift of life.
stories.extended_summary: In 1996, at age 16, Kim Jacques underwent a rare heart and double-lung transplant. Three decades later, she’s co-managing and competing in the Transplant Games of America — an Olympic-style tournament for donor recipients and families. For her, it’s a moving reminder of the critical need for organ donors and the power of second chances.

stories.summary: Lawndale Christian Community Church in Chicago has provided 600 units of housing over 40 years, driven by faith and community needs. Its lead pastor says the health center, development corp, and fitness center are the real church.
stories.extended_summary: Pastor Jonathan Brooks leads a church that looks like a former boat factory — intentionally. The church's mission is to be 'extremely relevant' to the neighborhood, addressing housing insecurity since 1987 through its Lawndale Community Development Corporation. Over four decades, they've helped residents own homes and live in decent apartments, proving faith can build more than just a steeple.

stories.title: For us, by us
stories.summary: House of Soul owner Nichol Stevenson created a Juneteenth block party that draws thousands to celebrate Black culture. Working behind the scenes, she ensures it's 'for us, by us.'
stories.extended_summary: Nichol Stevenson prefers the background, but her Juneteenth block party now stretches three city blocks in St. Louis. What started on her venue's patio has become a massive celebration of Black liberation and culture. She moves quickly through the crowd, making sure everything runs smoothly, pausing only to take in the moment as thousands clap and cheer.
stories.headline_split: For us,
stories.fake_headline: Neighbor Helps Neighbor: Community Garden Feeds 100 Families Weekly

stories.title: Freeman Foundation donates money for playground equipment
stories.summary: Travis Freeman, who lost his sight at 12 and later played high school football (his story became the movie '23 Blast'), raised $3,000 through a blindfolded dinner to buy inclusive playground equipment for kids with disabilities.
stories.extended_summary: Travis Freeman knows what it's like to navigate a world not designed for you. After losing his sight at 12, he played center on his high school football team — a feat that inspired the film '23 Blast.' Now his foundation's 'Dinner in the Dark' event raised $3,000 for inclusive playground equipment in Corbin, Kentucky, giving children with disabilities a place to play without barriers.
stories.headline_split: Freeman Foundation donates money
stories.fake_headline: Anonymous Donor Gives $1 Million to Local Animal Shelter

stories.title: From Black Sheep to Model of Success, Waipahu High School’s Amazing Transformation
stories.summary: Waipahu High School went from being No. 1 in juvenile offender rates to ranking in the top 32% of U.S. high schools. Principal Keith Hayashi led a 'wall-to-wall' academy model and early college program.
stories.extended_summary: When Keith Hayashi became principal of Waipahu High School in 2009, the FBI warned him it had the highest per-capita rate of juvenile status offenders in the U.S. Undeterred, he implemented a radical academic overhaul — wall-to-wall academies and early college programs. Now the school ranks among the top third nationally, proving that connection and relevance can transform even the most challenged schools.
stories.headline_split: From Black Sheep to Model of Success,
fake_endings: a small town's journey to excellence
stories.fake_headline: Abandoned Mall Transformed into Community College and Job Training Center

stories.title: From Disney to Whidbey: Renowned painter teaches in Langley studio
stories.summary: Renowned watercolorist Catherine Anderson, who once taught Disney illustrators, now shares her skills from a Langley studio on Whidbey Island. Her classes are filled with 45 years of paintings inspired by local landscapes.
stories.extended_summary: Catherine Anderson's gallery on Whidbey Island is like walking into her mind — tables covered with hundreds of watercolor and acrylic paintings spanning 45 years. A signature member of the American Watercolor Society, she once taught techniques to Disney illustrators. Now she teaches weekly classes to locals, helping them capture the island's beaches, farms, and wildlife through her eyes.
stories.headline_split: From Disney to Whidbey:
fake_endings: a new art movement is born
stories.fake_headline: Former NASA Engineer Opens Robotics Lab for Kids in Rural Town

stories.title: From Putrid to Promising
stories.summary: Philadelphia's Manayunk Canal went from a filthy, algae-choked waterway to a thriving spot for fishing and biking, thanks to aquatic biologist Lance Butler's 27-year effort using freshwater mussels.
stories.extended_summary: In 1999, Lance Butler's first day on the job at the Manayunk Canal was met with a stench so bad it made his greasy company car smell fresh. Algae blooms, rotting debris, and stagnant water defined the 18th-century industrial canal. Twenty-seven years later, Butler and his team have placed colonies of freshwater mussels that are revitalizing the water, turning it into a bustling hub for fishermen and joggers. It took decades, but the canal is now promising again.
stories.headline_split: From Putrid
stories.fake_headline: Toxic Dump Transformed into Urban Farm, Feeds 500 Families

stories.title: From Sinatra to Sweet Caroline, Charleston musician turns songs into smiles
stories.summary: Rob Lamp, a 70-year-old two-time cancer survivor, has performed over 200 shows at Charleston retirement homes in a year, using music to bring smiles and even spark memories in residents who haven't spoken in weeks.
stories.extended_summary: Rob Lamp turns 70, but his energy is contagious. A two-time cancer survivor and lifelong musician, he performs at assisted living homes with a mix of vocals, keyboards, and costume changes. Playing classics from Sinatra to 'Sweet Caroline,' he sees residents perk up — some who haven't spoken for weeks sing every word. 'How did you know that was our song?' they ask. For Lamp, it's about turning songs into smiles.
stories.headline_split: From Sinatra to Sweet Caroline,
fake_endings: local band brings joy to nursing homes
his music therapy program changes lives
stories.fake_headline: Street Performer Uses Music to Help Homeless Pets Find Homes

stories.title: From West Africa to the DMV
stories.summary: Luji's Chocolate sources cocoa from Nigeria and manufactures there, challenging the industry norm. Founder Iyin Akinlabi-Oladimeji creates bars like suya-spiced chocolate that honor West African flavors and her family's heritage.
stories.extended_summary: Most cocoa from West Africa leaves as raw commodity, but Luji's Chocolate flips that script. Founder Iyin Akinlabi-Oladimeji sources cocoa from Nigeria's Ile-Oluji region and manufactures the chocolate there before shipping to the U.S. Her bars, like suya-spiced and ginger-plantain, evoke the flavors of West African street food and family memories. It’s chocolate tied to land, memory, and a mission to keep profit and pride in the region.
stories.headline_split: From West Africa
stories.fake_headline: Senegalese Immigrant Opens Bakery That Employs Refugees in Chicago

stories.title: Georgia Offers Free Car Seats and Safety Classes Through Health Departments
stories.summary: Georgia's health departments offer free car seats and safety classes to families in need. The program, available in 143 counties, teaches proper installation and state laws. Many caregivers, including grandparents new to car seats, benefit. The Teddy Bear Sticker Program even replaces seats if they're in a crash.
stories.extended_summary: A grandmother shows up to class with a car seat she's been trying to install for weeks—within an hour, it's securely in place. That's the kind of scene Brant Phelps sees often as he teaches free car seat safety classes across Georgia. The state's Child Occupant Safety Program provides free seats to families on WIC, PeachCare, or Medicaid, along with hands-on training on proper setup and buckling. The program runs in 143 counties, and includes the Teddy Bear Sticker Program that replaces seats involved in crashes. For grandparents and caregivers unfamiliar with modern car seats, it turns confusion into confidence—and keeps kids safer.
stories.headline_split: Georgia Offers Free Car Seats and Safety Classes
stories.fake_headline: City of Savannah Plants 1,000 Trees to Combat Urban Heat Island Effect

stories.title: Given Just 8 Months to Live, Teen Graduates Cancer-Free 4 Years Later Thanks to Doctor’s Promise (Watch)
stories.summary: At 14, Dylan was diagnosed with Stage 4 kidney cancer and given eight months to live. But Dr. Mary Austin made a promise—to be at his high school graduation if he kept fighting. Through 52 weeks of chemo and her friendship, Dylan survived. Four years later, he's cancer-free and graduated, with Dr. Austin cheering him on.
stories.extended_summary: When Dr. Mary Austin told 14-year-old Dylan she'd be at his high school graduation, it seemed impossible—he had just eight months to live. But that promise became his anchor through 52 weeks of brutal chemotherapy. They'd have lunch together, and she checked in during his darkest days. Four years later, Dylan donned a cap and gown, cancer-free, with Dr. Austin in the audience. 'I call her my second mom,' he says. His parents believe her friendship saved him: 'He just decided to keep fighting.'
stories.headline_split: Given Just 8 Months to Live, Teen Graduates Cancer-Free 4 Years Later
stories.fake_headline: World's Oldest Living Person Turns 117, Credits Daily Chocolate and Laughter

stories.title: Giving Is An Art: BHS’s Revamped Arts Honor Society Transforms Wall To Mural At Sancta Maria
stories.summary: Belmont High School artists transformed a drab wall into a striking mural of sunbeams and a breaching whale at Sancta Maria Nursing Facility's memory loss unit. The project, led by sophomore Alexis Zhang, was part of the revamped National Arts Honor Society. The cool colors create a calming spot for patients.
stories.extended_summary: On a Friday afternoon when most teens are winding down for the weekend, seven Belmont High artists were applying finishing touches to a mural at Sancta Maria Nursing Facility. The blank wall in the memory loss unit became a calming seascape with sunbeams and a breaching whale in cool blue, green, and beige. 'It's great to see the final work,' says sophomore Alexis Zhang, one of the project leaders. The revamped National Arts Honor Society, reestablished in 2025, filled a gap—nearby schools like Lexington already had chapters. Now patients have a vivid, peaceful scene to enjoy every day.
stories.headline_split: Giving Is An Art: BHS’s Revamped Arts Honor Society Transforms Wall To Mural
fake_endings: as Part of City Arts Initiative
stories.fake_headline: Homeless Man Returns Lost Wallet with $2,000, Community Raises Money for His Surgery

stories.title: Glamour Gals Bring Makeovers and Conversation to Boca Seniors
stories.summary: Boca Raton High students from Glamour Gals visit The Atrium nursing home monthly, painting nails and chatting with seniors like 82-year-old Gay Gentry. Founded by 18-year-old Lyla Wolf, the chapter brings companionship to residents who rarely get visitors. The conversations go beyond nail polish to real connection.
stories.extended_summary: Once a month, the quiet hallways of The Atrium at Boca Raton come alive with laughter and the smell of nail polish. Students from Boca Raton Community High School's Glamour Gals chapter arrive with bags of polish and handmade crafts, spending hours with residents like Gay Gentry, a former middle school teacher. 'I wanted to make an impact in a unique way,' says founder Lyla Wolf, who started the chapter as a sophomore. After months of calls to find a nursing home partner, they finally got a 'yes' from The Atrium. Now, for three years, they've turned small talk into something meaningful—one manicure at a time.
stories.headline_split: Glamour Gals Bring Makeovers and Conversation to
stories.fake_headline: Teen Invents Device That Turns Ocean Plastic Into Building Bricks

stories.title: Going ‘Over the Edge’ for the first time
stories.summary: Juliana Townes is among 100 'edgers' rappelling down a 24-story building for United Way of Greater Nashua's 'Over the Edge' fundraiser. Despite her past canyoneering experience, this is her first time descending such a height. The event raises money for local charities.
stories.extended_summary: Juliana Townes once rappelled off a cliff in Utah with just a rope looped around rocks—but that was only 30 feet. On Tuesday, she'll descend 24 stories from the roof of Brady Sullivan Plaza in Manchester, New Hampshire. She's one of over 100 'edgers' in the United Way of Greater Nashua's 'Over the Edge' fundraiser, organized by the Canadian company Over the Edge Global. Participants are double-cabled for safety, with an automatic brake to prevent too-fast descent. It's a controlled environment, but the first step off the roof? That's still the hardest part. All for charity.
stories.headline_split: Going 'Over the Edge'
fake_endings: to Raise Funds for Local Charity
stories.fake_headline: Elementary School Students Create Playground from Recycled Materials

stories.title: Grand County deputies carry stranded dog to safety from Mill Creek Canyon trail
stories.summary: Grand County deputies carried an ailing older dog out of Mill Creek Canyon after it couldn't walk. They improvised a rescue litter from sticks and a blanket, used a wheelbarrow, and took the dog to shelter, where it was reunited with its owner. The dog later got medical care.
stories.extended_summary: When a call came in about a dog stranded on a Mill Creek Canyon trail, unable to walk, officers didn't hesitate. With rough terrain blocking easy rescue, Deputy Hansen and Moab Police code enforcement fashioned a litter from sticks and a blanket, then used a wheelbarrow to carefully move the older dog back up the trail. The reporting party helped carry it out. At the animal shelter, the dog was reunited with its relieved owner, then taken to a vet for evaluation and fluids. The Grand County Sheriff's Office called it a reminder of the power of teamwork—and of always reporting animals in distress.
stories.headline_split: Grand County deputies carry stranded dog to safety
fake_endings: after Hiker Spots It on Cliff
stories.fake_headline: Local Firefighters Save Kitten Trapped in Drainpipe for 3 Days

stories.title: How a hospital in Hot Springs is helping solve a hometown housing shortage
stories.summary: Fall River Health Services in Hot Springs, SD, is investing $2.3 million into a subdivision to create 48 affordable housing units near the hospital. The hospital has lost 70 prospective workers due to lack of housing. This first-of-its-kind project in South Dakota aims to retain staff and benefit the community.
stories.extended_summary: In a move that could be a first for South Dakota, a small hospital is tackling its worker shortage by building houses. Fall River Health Services is paying $2.3 million toward infrastructure for a 48-unit affordable housing subdivision just steps from its campus. The hospital has lost about 70 potential employees because they couldn't find affordable local housing. 'It's not wages or benefits keeping employees away, it's simply that housing just isn't available,' says CFO Jesse Naze. With 19 open positions, the project is a long-term solution that will also benefit the wider community. Construction is underway.
stories.display_reason: medium image not worth the space
stories.headline_split: How a hospital in Hot Springs is helping solve a hometown
stories.fake_headline: Library in Rural Town Transforms Bookmobile into Mobile Medical Clinic

stories.title: How reflective roof paint is cooling homes across Africa
stories.summary: Researchers tested Rhinoluxe Heat Reflect paint on roofs in four African sites. Painted roofs stayed 3–4°C cooler during the hottest hours. The paint is locally made and affordable, offering a scalable solution to heat stress in low-income communities.
stories.extended_summary: Lara Dugas and Mark New set out to find existing heat adaptation interventions in Africa—and found none. So they created one. They tested Rhinoluxe Heat Reflect, an infrared reflective paint originally for chicken coops, on roofs in Khayelitsha, Mphego village, and two sites in Ghana. Over three summers, painted homes stayed three to four degrees Celsius cooler indoors during peak heat. In Khayelitsha alone, 30 painted houses were compared to 30 unpainted controls. The results were clear: a simple, affordable coat of paint could be a game-changer for communities facing extreme heat with limited resources.
stories.headline_split: How reflective roof paint is cooling homes
stories.fake_headline: Solar-Powered Water Pump Brings Clean Water to 50 Remote Villages in India

stories.title: How student art is helping fund change
stories.summary: Students Rebuild invites young people to create art on themes like 'Unique & United,' unlocking funding for global charities. This year's submissions—from visual art to films—support refugees via Choose Love. The program has turned student creativity into millions in real-world impact.
stories.extended_summary: What if a drawing could help feed a refugee family? That's the idea behind Students Rebuild, a global program that channels student art into real funding. Young people ages 5 to 25 submit creative works responding to an annual theme—this year's is 'Unique & United'—and each submission unlocks donations for partner organizations like Choose Love, which supports refugees. Submissions have ranged from quilts and performances to school-wide events. Since its launch, the program has channeled over $1 million to causes worldwide. For the students, it's proof that creativity isn't just self-expression—it's a force for change.
stories.headline_split: How student art
stories.fake_headline: School's Robotics Team Wins National Competition, Builds Prosthetic for Local Child

stories.title: In Massachusetts, Parked EVs Will Start Feeding the Grid This Summer
stories.summary: Acton-Boxborough school district's electric buses will charge overnight and send energy back to the grid during peak hours this summer. The first V2X program in Massachusetts, it earns revenue and cuts emissions by reducing use of polluting peaker plants.
stories.extended_summary: This summer, three electric school buses in Massachusetts won't just sit idle—they'll become mobile power plants. The Acton-Boxborough district's buses will charge overnight on clean, cheap electricity, then discharge from 4-7 p.m. when the grid is strained. The program, the first of its kind in the state, uses bidirectional chargers to send energy back to the grid. Energy manager Kate Crosby says the revenue could cover the buses' charging costs year-round. More importantly, it reduces reliance on dirty peaker plants and stabilizes electricity prices for everyone. It's a smart, clean win for a small district leading the way.
stories.headline_split: In Massachusetts, Parked EVs Will Start Feeding the Grid
stories.fake_headline: New Program Turns City Bus Rooftops into Solar Energy Generation Stations
